context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 5

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 5


Problem 5.1 (20 pts)

In a lab, a graduate student was studying a string of length L, fixed at both ends.

(a) Find the total energy of a vibration of the string oscillating with its nth normal mode with amplitude A. The tension in the string is T and its total mass is M.
    Hint: Consider the integrated kinetic energy at the instant when the string is straight, such that the potential energy due to the vibration is zero.
(b) Calculate the total energy of vibration of the same string if it is vibrating in the following superposition of normal modes. Assume that it is the sum of the energies of the two modes taken separately.
    ψ(x,t) = A1·sin(πx/L)·cos(ω1·t) + A3·sin(3πx/L)·cos(ω3·t − π/4)


Problem 5.2 (20 pts)

A string of length L, tension T, and mass per unit length ρL fixed at both ends is distorted as shown in Figure 1. The height of the square pulse is h. The string is constrained to vibrate only in the vertical direction. At t = 0, the string is carefully released such that the initial velocity of the string is 0.

[Figure 1: A string fixed at x = 0 and x = L, with a square pulse of height h and width L/4 centered at x = L/2.]

(a) What is the speed of a progressing wave on this string?
(b) Plot to scale the shape of the string at t = (L/4)·√(ρL/T).
(c) Plot to scale the shape of the string at t = L·√(ρL/T).


Problem 5.3 (20 pts)

Consider a string of mass density ρ under tension T. A pulse is traveling on the string, causing a vertical displacement given by:
    y(x,t) = y0·exp[ −(1/2)·((x − vt)/σ)^2 ]
Energy in the wave is a sum of the kinetic energy of the moving string and the potential energy of the string deformation, integrated over the whole length of the string.

(a) For the displacement given above, find an expression for the kinetic energy density of the string at some time t and sketch the result as a function of x.
(b) Find an expression for the potential energy density of the string. Compare it with the kinetic energy density.
(c) Find an expression for the total energy of the pulse in terms of T, y0 and σ. Check the units. Recall that the energy should vary as the square of the amplitude.
    Hint: You may need to use the Gaussian integral ∫_{−∞}^{∞} x^2·e^(−a·x^2) dx = (1/2a)·√(π/a).
(d) Find an expression for the power (Watts) flowing across the point x = x0 as a function of time. Sketch the result.


Problem 5.4 (20 pts)

Consider a string with linear density ρL split into two pieces. The two halves are attached to a massless ring which slides vertically without friction on a rod at x = 0 as shown in Figure 2. One of the two halves is stretched in the negative x direction with tension TL, the other is stretched in the positive x direction with tension TR. Note that the vertical rod is necessary to balance the horizontal forces on the massless string arising from the two strings with different tensions.
Assume that there is a traveling wave of amplitude A coming from the negative x direction.

[Figure 2: Two string halves with a massless ring at x = 0 — the left half under tension TL, the right half under tension TR, joined at a ring that slides on a vertical rod.]

(a) Assuming the incident wave has the functional form ψ(x,t) = α·cos(kx − ωt), write the general expression for the string displacement ψ(x,t) on both sides of the ring.
(b) Write the expressions for the boundary conditions at x = 0. (Hint: since the ring is massless, the total force on the ring is 0.)
(c) Find the reflection (R) and transmission (T) coefficients for this system.
(d) Show that the energy is conserved at the junction.
(e) What are the reflection and transmission coefficients in the following three cases: TR = TL, TR ≫ TL, and TR ≪ TL? Does your result make sense?


Problem 5.5 (20 pts)

A string of mass density ρL under tension T is attached by a massless ring to a wire covered with a viscous grease. The ring experiences a vertical drag force Fy = −b·(∂y/∂t) when the end of the string moves.

[Figure: the end of the string attached to a ring on a wire covered with grease, providing a viscous drag force.]

(a) Use Newton's Law applied to the ring to find the boundary condition at the end of the string as a relation between the partial derivatives of y(x,t) at that point. (Hint: there are three forces acting on the ring: string tension, normal force from the wire, and the vertical drag force.)
(b) Show that the boundary condition is satisfied by an incident pulse f(t − x/v) and reflected pulse g(t + x/v). Find g in terms of f.
(c) Show that your result has the proper behavior in the limits b → 0 (free slip) and b → ∞ (clamped string).

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on Problem 5.1, especially the energy part for a vibrating string. Could we start there, but maybe not just jump straight to the formula because I’m not even sure why the hint says to look when the string is straight?
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point. Think about a simpler system first: a mass on a spring. At what point in its oscillation is the kinetic energy at its maximum, and what is the potential energy doing at that exact moment?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking about the hint in 5.1(a) — why we evaluate kinetic energy when the string is straight. This is a conceptual entry point. Rather than explaining it, I should ask a Socratic question that leads them to think about energy conservation and the relationship between potential and kinetic energy at specific moments in the oscillation. I'll keep it brief and let them reason it out.

exchanges.student: I think the kinetic energy is biggest when it passes through equilibrium, and the spring potential energy is zero there. I’m not sure how that transfers to a whole string though, since different parts of the string are moving differently.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right for the mass-spring case. Now, for the string: when the string is perfectly straight, what can you say about its deformation — and therefore about the potential energy stored in that deformation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the mass-spring analogy and is now questioning how it scales to a continuous system. This is good progress. I should affirm the insight and push them to think about what 'equilibrium' looks like for a vibrating string — i.e., when the string is straight — and what that implies for the potential energy stored in the string's deformation. A single guiding question is enough.

exchanges.student: I guess if it’s straight then there’s no extra stretching from the vibration, so the vibration potential energy should be zero then too. But then I immediately get stuck because I feel like I need the full derivation for the nth mode and I’m fuzzy on what expression to use for the velocity of the string elements.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've got the key insight. So now, what does the displacement of the string look like for the nth normal mode? Try writing it down, even roughly, and then we can think about what the velocity of each string element would be.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly reasoned that the potential energy is zero when the string is straight, which is the key insight from the hint. Now they're stuck on the velocity expression. I should guide them to write down the nth normal mode displacement first, and then think about what the time derivative gives them — rather than handing them the velocity expression. One targeted question is enough.

exchanges.student: I think it’s something like \(y(x,t)=A\sin(n\pi x/L)\cos(\omega t)\), but I’m not confident if that’s exactly right. And I’m tempted to ask for the whole setup at once because I keep losing track of which quantity gets squared and integrated.
exchanges.tutor: That expression is correct. Now, kinetic energy involves velocity — so what do you get when you take the time derivative of that displacement? Just focus on that one step for now.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly written the nth normal mode displacement. I should affirm this and then ask them to take the next logical step: compute the time derivative to get the velocity. I should not give them the full setup — instead, push them to do one step at a time. Their concern about 'which quantity gets squared and integrated' will resolve naturally once they write down the velocity expression and connect it to kinetic energy.

exchanges.student: I get \( \partial y/\partial t = -A\omega \sin(n\pi x/L)\sin(\omega t)\). I’m still feeling like I only half-see it, though; could you maybe just show the full energy expression after this step so I can connect the pieces?
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right. I won't give you the full expression — that's your job! But you're closer than you think. You have the velocity of each string element. How would you write the kinetic energy of a tiny piece of string with mass dm and that velocity?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly computed the time derivative. They're asking me to show the full energy expression, which I must refuse per the rules. Instead, I should acknowledge their correct answer and push them to the next step: writing down the kinetic energy density using their velocity expression. This is a natural next step that they can do themselves.

exchanges.student: I think \(dK=\tfrac12\,dm\,(\partial y/\partial t)^2\), and then maybe \(dm=(M/L)\,dx\). That didn’t really clear it up for me though; I’m still not seeing how the nth mode changes the final answer, and I’m worried I’m missing some big-picture shortcut.
exchanges.tutor: You have all the pieces. Try writing out the full integral for the total kinetic energy by substituting your expressions for dm and ∂y/∂t. What does that integral look like explicitly? Don't simplify yet — just write it out.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly set up the kinetic energy element. They now need to put it all together by substituting their velocity expression and integrating. The 'shortcut' they're worried about missing is just the orthogonality integral of sin², which gives L/2. I should push them to write out the full integral rather than hinting at the shortcut — let them discover it. One targeted question.

exchanges.student:
I think it’s
\[
K=\frac12\int_0^L \frac{M}{L}\left(A\omega \sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right)\sin(\omega t)\right)^2 dx.
\]
I can write that, but I’m still kind of hoping for a more general explanation of the whole problem since part (b) also has superposed modes and I don’t want to get trapped in just this one algebra step.
exchanges.tutor: You're making real progress — don't jump ahead yet. Part (b) will make much more sense once you see what happens here. What is the value of ∫₀ᴸ sin²(nπx/L) dx? That's the key step standing between you and the answer for part (a).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly wrote the full integral. They're now asking for a broader explanation to avoid getting stuck — this is a reasonable concern but I should not give them the big picture directly. The key next step is evaluating the sin² integral, which will reveal the L/2 result and then naturally connect to part (b) via the orthogonality of different modes. I should push them to evaluate the integral now — one step at a time — and trust that the connection to part (b) will become clearer. I should also remind them we're making good progress and discourage detours.

exchanges.student: I think it’s \(L/2\), but I’m not fully sure if that stays true for every integer \(n\). If that’s right, then I’m guessing the total energy ends up depending on \(A^2\omega^2 M\), but I still want help seeing the bigger pattern before I mess up part (b).
exchanges.tutor: Correct — L/2 holds for any nonzero integer n. You're very close to the answer for part (a). Remember this is the kinetic energy at one instant — how does that relate to the *total* energy of the oscillation? Finish writing the complete expression for part (a), and the pattern for part (b) will follow naturally.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the integral as L/2 (valid for all nonzero integers n) and is correctly anticipating the form of the answer. They're asking for the 'bigger pattern' before moving to part (b), which is a reasonable concern but I should not give it to them — instead, push them to finish part (a) by writing the complete expression, and then the pattern for part (b) will emerge naturally. I should also note that ω depends on n, which they'll need.

exchanges.student: So I think the total energy for one mode is the same as that maximum kinetic energy, giving \(E=\tfrac14 M A^2\omega_n^2\). I’m still uneasy because for part (b) I kind of want the general rule spelled out for superpositions instead of having to infer it.
exchanges.tutor: Correct — well done on part (a). For part (b), don't look for a general rule yet. Just try writing out the kinetic energy integral for the superposition ψ given in the problem, the same way you did for part (a). What happens to the cross terms when you integrate?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly arrived at the total energy for the nth mode. Now they want the 'general rule' for superpositions handed to them before attempting part (b). I should not give this to them. Instead, I should acknowledge their correct answer for part (a) and push them to think about what happens when you add two modes — specifically, whether cross terms survive the integration. This is the key insight they need to discover themselves.

exchanges.student: I think the cross terms have something like \(\sin(\pi x/L)\sin(3\pi x/L)\), so maybe they integrate to zero by orthogonality. I’m not totally convinced, and honestly I’d really like a broad summary of the method for the whole homework because I keep feeling lost from problem to problem.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — orthogonality kills the cross terms, and that's precisely why the problem tells you to assume the total energy is the sum of the two modes separately. I won't summarize the whole homework — my role is to guide you through each problem as you work it. You now have everything needed for parts (a) and (b). Try writing up your complete solution for both parts and share it here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the orthogonality of the cross terms, which is the key insight for part (b). I should affirm this and push them to write the complete answer for part (b) now. However, they're also asking for a broad summary of the whole homework, which is outside the scope of what I should provide — I should redirect them back to the problem at hand. We've also had several exchanges building up to this point, so I should ask them to integrate what we've discussed into a written solution attempt.
